speaker: interviewer
text: Eva Kor. And the date is April 2, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, before we continue, I'd like to ask you the length of time that you spent in the ghetto with your family, and then the length of time that you spent in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I again, I will have to estimate. I never had a calendar. Nor did I think at the time that it would be something that I would need to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I know that about five weeks we were in the ghetto. Now, the question is did we arrive in Auschwitz in April, end of April, or early May? Either way, I was in the camp for nine months or a maximum of 10. But I would say, for the sake of being closer, to nine months. And then from there on, thereabout from January 27th-- which is the end of January-- until October-- late September or early October.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I know, it was after school started. And it was getting cold outside. And from what memories go back to, I think it was probably early October. We were-- so that would be another nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were away from home about a total of one and a half years. And when we arrived in Slucz, it was a regular DP camp like all the other DP camps. We would get a little bit of money, about half a ruble every week, from the Soviet. We could go out into the village to the open market and buy some food, [INAUDIBLE] fruit in particular. And I always liking to explore the surrounding. I went around. It was told to us that this was one time a ghetto. And there was a very unusual hill in Slucz. It was a U-shape hill, very tall. And it had a lot of beautiful flowers-- red flowers, spring flowers. And I said, well, wouldn't that be nice if I climbed all the way to the top and collected a bouquet for Mrs. Changeri for thanking her for taking care of us. I did. And I collected a beautiful bouquet. I did find something strange. As I was climbing that hill, that there were huge chunks of bones sticking out. I didn't know what to think, nor did I understand. But I did notice that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got back to the barrack, and I said to Mrs. Changeri, here, I want to thank you for taking care of us-- beautiful wildflowers. And she said, oh, very pretty. Thank you. Where did you get them? And I said, well, I went up to that U-shaped hill. And they were right on the top. And I wanted to climb all the way to the top just to bring them to you. She said, don't you ever go back there again. And I said, why? She said, well, don't you know? This is a hill that was created when the Minsk Nazis-- Minsk in Belarussia-- were running out of ammunition. They gathered the people in the ghetto. And they ran out of bullets. So they buried them alive and watched them for three days and nights. These hills were going up and down. I don't know if she found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interestingly enough, she was right, because I read about a book by John Loftus, and it talks about the situation in some of the ghettos in Belarussia where they could not have enough ammunition. And people were buried alive. So I learned another little aspect of the Holocaust story. To me, that was something new I didn't know about. We stayed there for about-- the big interesting thing was there that there were a lot of other prisoners. Like I remember Italians who  were held in that camp, were prisoners of war. And they would be very interested in entertaining and singing. And they would put on little shows and make stages out of blanket. And then, of course, I realized that many of the women were going out with these guys. I was just watching. Life was for them becoming somewhat of a normal thing. It was from my point of view, it was an interesting observation. I didn't quite understand everything. One day, we were put on the train. And his train was moving slowly, just like all the trains after the war, because the war was still going on in many places. And we could see enormous destruction. I mean, there were complete villages completely wiped out by bombing. And we came back to Romania. And we entered with Mrs. [INAUDIBLE] with Mrs. Changeri in Nagyvarad or Oradea. And there was-- in our group there was one-- Mrs. Changeri was together with another lady by the name of Goldental. Mrs. Goldental had two twin boys and one little girl-- Margarita, who was three years old, that she hid under her skirt when she was brought to the camp. She doesn't have a number. And she was always kept under the mattress when the inspections were done. And she-- I met her. I saw her in Israel a few years ago. But I remember her clearly. And so Mrs. Godental and Mrs. Changeri and us-- we were-- we arrived back in Oradea. Mrs. Goldental was from Oradea. So she stayed there. Mrs. Changeri and us continued on to [NON-ENGLISH]. And we stayed one night with Mrs. Changeri. And then a cousin who has survived-- my father's oldest daughter Sarah. He was the only one from 13 children to survive. And his parents died. My uncle and aunt died. And he returned early-- earlier. And they were very poor. They usually, my parents always helped them. So he came to the farm to try to grow some wheat and food, because Romania after the war was-- had no food whatsoever. And so my aunt-- and I didn't know that they knew about. There were-- the Israel-- the Jewish [INAUDIBLE] with the Red Cross had a complete list of transports for people coming back. So my aunt knew a week in advance that we were on that train coming back. And my cousin came to [NON-ENGLISH] to get us to put us on the train to go back home. Well, I was very excited we were going back home. I didn't know what I would find. It was probably one of the most disappointing moment. During the time that I was in the camp, there was almost like a slogan that we would get to say every day-- someday soon we will be free, and we will go home. And we know the airplanes were coming. So we would be free. The picture of home that I had in my mind was my mother, my father, my two older sisters with the twins. And that picture was never again going to be realized. The house was completely ransacked. The weeds were tall. There were-- was one bed in one room that my cousin got from somewhere so he could sleep on. The floors were dirty. And there were three crumbled pictures in one of the bedrooms that I picked up. And there was my mother's dog, Lilly, was still alive. And I looked at him. And I said, gosh, I guess Jewish dogs are not taken to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not want to stay there very long. It was more difficult to stay in a place that I was hoping to see in the conditions that it was. And so my aunt anyway wanted us to move to the city to Kolozsvar-- Cluj. And the cousin took us by train to my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to your home, did you see any of your old neighbors or any of your childhood friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter a few of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't even stay longer than one day. I could not cope with the desolate. The place was so haunted, so empty, that there was no-- no reason for us to stay there. And-- so no, I could not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard that the people were still anti-Semitic and that it was dangerous to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know if they were anti-Semitic. But I do know that years later, when I went back the following summer for summer vacation, and-- and I went to see the neighbors. And I was in a little bit better shape and talking, they said, well, we couldn't do anything. We-- I saw my mother's towels, tablecloths, because mother had everything monogrammed. And I would look at it. And I would recognize it and say, oh, yeah, I just took that to save it for you when you got back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think they were very glad that we were taken away. I think that they did not want us to return. It got very bad toward 1948 and '49 and '50. That when we finally got our visa to immigrate to Israel, I did want to go back and maybe just see the house, maybe find my grandfather's grave. But we knew that there were all kind of stories of people that are attacking former landowners, and that it was not wise for us to go back to the village, because we might be attacked. The only reason that Miriam and I were not put on trial, because we were war orphans. We were not jailed. Many Jewish people who returned from the camp, who were grownups, were put on trial and jailed. Life in-- in Hungary and Romania-- or in Cluj-- it was very difficult. Poverty was everywhere. Food very scarce. Number one, we were so malnutritioned, we had so many health problems, it was unbelievable. We would get little spots-- dots-- like little bubbles on our skin. And it would grow into the size of a quarter, and at times, even an apple. And my aunt didn't know what to do with us. It was very painful all over my hand, leg, stomach, everywhere. My aunt took us to the doctor. And the doctor said, these children suffer from so much malnutrition. That these are blisters of malnutrition. Get them anything that you can to eat. 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to the synagogue one evening. And the rabbi said it was Friday night. Sunday we are going to have a memorial-- first memorial service for the people who were killed in the death camps. They didn't call it Holocaust then. And if anybody has any soap from the camp, to please bring it with you to the service, because we are going to bury it since it was made out of human fat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I still had two bars of soap. I knew from the time before we went to the camp that soap was very difficult to find. And it was a luxurious commodity. And my mother said, you have to save all the soap. So when I was in the camp, we got a bar of soap once a week. I kept with me the soap. I-- I did not talk to it about it to my sister, or to my aunt, or to my uncle, because my aunt married somebody who lost all his family. I just developed-- I took the soap. We buried it. And I developed severe nightmares.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was dreaming nightly that I was washing with soap. And the soap would turn into the voices of my parents. And they would yell at me, why do you wash with me? I would break out in a terrible sweat and began screaming. And I would wake up. I could not wash with soap for years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even talk to Miriam about it. We still were not at the point where I could communicate with anybody any of my feelings, any of my thoughts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, the nightmares ended when we went to Israel. However, there is one little story that you probably-- since you asked me about anti-Semitism-- yes, there sure was anti-Semitism. There was a rumor-- in Kolozsvar-- Cluj-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, first of all, we lived with my aunt. But we had very little food. We ate in an orphanage. So we would go there at noon, eat something, and then we would go back to school, and we would go there at night and eat dinner there, or whatever it was. And we would take some milk for breakfast home. Well the rumor was that there was a vampire-- a Jewish vampire-- who was attacking people on the street. Since we had to walk after school, it was quite a distance to the orphanage to get our food and our milk, because you couldn't get milk any other way. And because we had so much malnutrition problems, we needed to get the milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kept wondering how-- I worried, well, does this vampire know that I am Jewish, so he wouldn't attack me? As a child, I didn't question the fact that this was fabricated by somebody. But I was worried that he was going to attack me, that he wouldn't know that I was Jewish. Yes, there were a lot of other problems, even though the slogans of the communist were brotherhood, equality.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did-- they did do a very interesting things. They put me and my sister in charge of the Youth Communist Party in our school. Here, all of a sudden, I was a big shot. I was deciding what young-- who is going to join the Communist Party. And I would ask them questions. And I was very active. And I was very enthusiastic. That's the first time in my life that anybody said that  I was equal to them. But I was always very independent. And on a-- in 1948 in a May Day parade, I was leading of 30 pioneers, as well as my sister had with 30 pioneers. We were supposed to go in the morning to a May Day parade. Then lunch we had picnic in the park. And then we had a torch parade in the evening. And the next day, we had school. And according quote to the Communist Party, children, the students, had to be the best communist-- had to be the best student in their class. And so I just very simply said to my students, you have a test tomorrow. We are not going to the torch parade. You go home and study. It's more important to study. The next  day, I was called in to the Communist Party. I was 14-- 13, 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this, I remember it clearly, from in the center of Cluj, the second story above a movie house. And I'm going, and I'm after all, I'm an Auschwitz survivor. What can happen to me, right? And the guy starts hitting the table-- the commandant, the chief of the, or the director of the Communist Party in the city. Where were you yesterday with your troop? And I said, well, we are supposed to be the best students in class. So I told the kids after spending the morning in the parade, the noon in the park, I thought they needed to study. He started hitting the table. You stupid idiot! Don't you know you're not supposed to think! You are just supposed to follow orders. I said, OK, if I cannot think, I don't want to be in your party. I mean, I thought to myself, what can they-- and I said, so what can you do to me? He said, you're stupid. You are very stupid. You really think you're so smart. He said, yeah, you don't want to be in the Communist Party? You cannot attend any school in Romania. Is that clear to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started bothering me, because I was 13 years old. And my whole life was going to be over without an education. So I followed the orders. And I was petrified. In 1948, when Israel became a country, my aunt applied for a visa. Now, we had a big problem. And I found out again what the problem is. Nobody want old people. Everybody wants young people. My aunt had her own name. And she married a guy in the synagogue, which the Romanian government did not recognize. His name was [? Hirsch, ?] not Hersh-- [? Hirsch. ?] And he lost the wife and daughter-- two daughter in the camp. But actually, what happened with my uncle, he was a pharmacist. And in 1948, shortly after we applied for a visa, he disappeared. One day as the secret police came, they picked up my uncle. And we didn't know where he was, if he was alive or dead. We didn't know what happened to him. My aunt applied for a visa. The Romanians wanted to give her a visa. The Israelis didn't want to give her an entry visa. The Romanians didn't want to give us an exit visa. The Israelis wanted. Everybody wants the young people. Nobody wants the old. That's it. After two years, first of all, they said, well, we could go. We take our furniture, our clothing, sheets, blankets, whatever. I remember for about a year and a half having most of the furniture packed up in huge boxes ready to go. You could just open the box and get ready to go. There was a stupid scale on the kitchen table that we would weigh every clothing item, because there was only so many kilograms you could take. Then the order came that you could take only 15 kilogram above, which means the furniture couldn't go anymore. When we finally got our visa, my uncle was released only to join  us. We were only-- we could take only what we could wear. We got on the ship. And we sailed for Israel. When the ship was far enough from the Romanian border, my uncle said to us, I was put in jail. I was a communist all my life. I worked with the Romanian underground to help bring communism to Romania. But he said, it doesn't work. Once they got into power, every single one of them was trying to make money for himself without worrying about the ideals of what we worked for. He said, he was running a pharmacy for the Communist Party. All the profits were going to the Communist Party. And he said he  was going to expose the people who were pocketing the money. And in order to shut him up, they put him in jail. The only reason they let him out is to leave Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, children, it's my opinion that if you don't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believe in communism, or you don't want communism when you are 16, you don't have a heart. But if you still want communism when you are 26, you don't have a brain, because he said, the ideals of it are beautiful. But it just doesn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Israel. The-- ship-- I think were on the ocean-- I don't know-- five days maybe-- three days. We never saw the-- the cabins. We were always on the top of the deck. That ship was meant for 1,000. It was loaded with 3,000 immigrants-- all survivors of camps. And lucky for us, because after our ship, they closed down the-- the gates or Romania for two years. The ship pulled up in Haifa early morning. And I think on purpose it was designed that way. People started singing the 'Hatikvah.' And the sun-- if you've ever been to Haifa, Mt. Carmel is there. And the sun in June, it's always sunny. The sun was rising. And the rays were high above. It's like a golden globe surrounding us. It was like-- it was the most beautiful thing that I can remember. It was just like welcoming us. Many people got off the ship, kissed the ground crying. My uncle who was working in the port came up to us. And actually, I don't remember him before he left, because we were just born. And we hugged him and kissed him. And he was very, very nice. And he said, that he would visit us. We have to be in quarantine for a while. And he would meet with us when he can. And we were taken-- my aunt and uncle were taken to a grown up quarentine. We were taken to [? Yusalia. ?] I didn't know what that was. But there were lots of children. And we were taken to a village named Magdiel near Ramat Gan near Kwasaba-- between Ramat Gan and Kwasaba. And the-- the first night we arrived there was a Friday. Well, everybody was going to the dining room with white-- gleaming white shirts. And we're saying the [NON-ENGLISH] and kiddush with the wine, and singing all kind of Israeli songs. And it was absolutely-- I couldn't believe that there was that many Jewish people in one place. That night after the Shabbat services and the dinner, we all went to a big, huge hall where everybody was dancing all kind of Israeli dances. Two girls were assigned to us to kind of teach us how to dance and start. And it-- it was I was in [? Yusalia ?] for two years. And I can honestly say that it was the only two years that I had a childhood. I never feared going to sleep, or being called a dirty Jew, or worrying about anything. I became a milkmaid, because I saw there were lots of young guys. And I definitely was interested. It was an age that I was very interested to be different and to work with guys. And my sister worked-- we worked for a while in the field picking tomatoes and peanuts. And Miriam worked in the kitchen, as well as in the farm. But those who worked as milkmaids could stay there all the time. They didn't have to be rotated, because you had to learn how to milk the cows. We went to school half a day. And we learned Hebrew. And I learned Hebrew very fast. In three months, I could speak Hebrew. And then in 1952, we were drafted into the Israeli Army. Up to that time, I did not really feel very different because in that [NON-ENGLISH] village, there were children from 30 countries. I learned to say I love you in 10 languages. And we all were children who have been traumatized in one way or another by World War II. But once I was in the Israeli Army, there  were young people-- soldiers, women-- who  had families in Tel Aviv, who could home after finishing work, or on weekends. I had no place to go. And that became very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, Miriam and I got separated. She studied to become a nurse. Because one of my cousins said, well, you know, growing up as a man, I look at it that for any man is difficult to deal with one woman. How are they going to deal with two of them alike? So you ought to go your different ways-- separate ways. So she started to become a nurse. And I went to the engineering corps. And it's almost like losing part of me. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time we met after being separated for three weeks, I went to the hospital where she was studying. We cried for an hour. We just couldn't-- couldn't stop crying. But I think it was good for us to become-- develop our own personalities. I had a very tough time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were together with your sister, did you ever talk about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nothing from before the war. Nothing from during the war never came up. And the reason I think very clearly to me today is that it was a situation that we probably emotionally couldn't cope with the pain. It wasn't anything that I was always very proud that I survived. But I think dealing with that pain, it just-- she never brought it up. And I never brought it up. People would say, oh, you have a number. I said, yeah. I survived Mengele's experiments. Nobody ever asked me more. I don't know if they did how I would have handled it. But I never felt that I was doing anything that didn't answer their question. I answered it very matter of fact. There was never any emotion involved in it. That is true. It was just-- we were used in medical experiment. Yes, my family was killed. Very matter of fact. It was almost like I was just giving a report.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have dreams at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not after I lived in Israel. No. It's almost like I started a brand-new slate in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
